title: Multithreading & Concurrency
objectives: Understand multithreading through a real-life analogy
Distinguish threads, executors, and virtual threads
Master the classic bugs: race conditions and deadlocks — and their fixes
Understand the Java Memory Model + happens-before

sections.markdown:
**Imagine a coffee shop with three baristas.**

- **One barista** taking every order = single-threaded. Simple. Everyone in line waits.
- **Three baristas** working in parallel = multi-threaded. Faster… but now they might both grab the last cup of milk. That's a **race condition**.
- To avoid the milk fight, they use a **lock** on the milk fridge: one at a time, please.
- If barista A holds the milk and waits for the espresso machine, and barista B holds the espresso machine and waits for the milk, nobody can move. That's a **deadlock**.
- A **manager (thread pool)** decides how many baristas exist and reuses them for many orders — better than hiring one per order.

**Multithreading is coordinating multiple workers on shared resources.** Get the coordination right and you're fast. Get it wrong and you get subtle bugs that only show up under load.

**Multithreading** is running multiple threads of execution concurrently within one process. They share memory — which makes communication cheap but synchronization necessary. Java's concurrency toolkit has evolved dramatically: raw `Thread`, then `ExecutorService`, then `CompletableFuture`, and finally virtual threads (Java 21).
## Three ways to create a thread — old to modern
## The concurrency toolkit — what to reach for

rows: Exclusive lock on an object monitor
More flexible lock — tryLock, timeout, fairness

sections.markdown:
## The two classic bugs — race conditions and deadlocks

Every multithreading interview asks about these. If you can explain both with examples, you're 80% of the way there.

**Race condition — counter++ isn't atomic.**

`count++` looks like one operation. It's actually three: read count, add 1, write count. If two threads interleave those three steps, one update is lost.

**Fixes** (in order of preference): `AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()`, or wrap the increment in `synchronized`, or (best) don't share the state.

**Deadlock — two locks, opposite order.**

```
Thread A: lock(X); lock(Y);   // waits for Y
Thread B: lock(Y); lock(X);   // waits for X
// Both wait forever. App freezes.
```

**Fix:** always acquire locks in a **globally consistent order** — every thread grabs X before Y. No cycles form.

## `volatile` vs `synchronized` vs `Atomic` — the interview trap

Most beginners think `volatile` makes `count++` thread-safe. It doesn't.

rows: Mutual exclusion (one thread at a time)

sections.markdown: **`volatile int done = false;`** is fine for a done-flag (visibility is enough). **`volatile int count; count++;`** is still buggy — because count++ isn't atomic. Use `AtomicInteger` for that.

## The Java Memory Model in one paragraph

Without synchronization, the JVM can **reorder writes** and **cache values in CPU registers** — two threads may see different values for the same variable. The **JMM (Java Memory Model)** defines a **happens-before** relationship: certain constructs — a `synchronized` release/acquire, `volatile` write/read, `Thread.start`/`join`, atomic operations — guarantee that all writes before them are visible to any thread that observes them. If you don't establish happens-before, you're on your own.
## A complete sample program — race demo, then all the fixes
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Believing `volatile` makes `count++` safe.**

`volatile` only guarantees visibility, not atomicity. `count++` = read, add, write — three separate ops. Use `AtomicInteger` or `synchronized`.

**Mistake 2: `new Thread()` per task in server code.**

Each OS thread costs ~1 MB of stack and setup time. Spawning one per HTTP request kills servers. Use a thread pool (`Executors.newFixedThreadPool`) — or virtual threads in Java 21.

**Mistake 3: Holding a lock across an I/O call.**

Don't call the network or a slow DB while holding a lock — you serialize all other threads waiting on that lock. Copy what you need, release the lock, then do the I/O.
## Best practices
**1. Prefer executors and virtual threads over raw `Thread`.** Modern Java has better primitives.
**2. Minimize shared mutable state.** Immutable data + message passing (via `BlockingQueue` or `CompletableFuture`) eliminates whole categories of bugs.
**3. Use `ConcurrentHashMap` instead of `Collections.synchronizedMap`.** Fine-grained locking is much faster under contention.
**4. Prefer `CompletableFuture` for async pipelines** — chaining `.thenApply` / `.thenCompose` is clearer than nested callbacks.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — See a race live.** Spawn 8 threads incrementing a shared `int` 100,000 times each. Print the final value. It won't be 800,000. Fix with `AtomicInteger`.

**Why:** You'll never forget what a race feels like.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — CompletableFuture pipeline.** Fetch a user, then their orders, then their most-expensive order — all async, in one chain: `.thenCompose(...).thenApply(...)`.

**Why:** This is how modern async Java is written.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Producer/consumer with BlockingQueue.** One producer thread puts numbers into a bounded queue; two consumer threads read + process. Verify no items are lost.

**Why:** Every real background pipeline (image resizing, log ingestion) is this shape.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Build a mini thread pool from scratch.** With a `BlockingQueue<Runnable>` and N worker threads. Match the API of `ExecutorService.submit`.

**Why:** You'll internalize how a thread pool actually works.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What's the difference between `volatile`, `synchronized`, and `AtomicInteger`?**

**A:** All three ensure **visibility** — writes by one thread are visible to another. But they differ on **atomicity** and **mutual exclusion**. **`volatile`** guarantees visibility only. Every read reaches memory (not a stale cached copy) and every write flushes. But `count++` still races because it's read-modify-write. Great for done-flags or one-off state changes; wrong for counters. **`synchronized`** provides visibility AND mutual exclusion via an object monitor — one thread at a time. Slower under contention. **`AtomicInteger`** uses hardware Compare-And-Swap (CAS) instructions — lock-free, very fast, atomic read-modify-write. Rule: `volatile` for flags, `Atomic*` for counters, `synchronized` for compound invariants across multiple fields.

**Q2: What is the Java Memory Model, and what does 'happens-before' mean?**

**A:** The Java Memory Model (JMM) defines when writes made by one thread become visible to another. Without it, the compiler and CPU are free to reorder operations and cache values in registers — two threads may see completely different values for the same variable. The JMM establishes a **happens-before** relationship: if action A happens-before action B, then everything visible after A is visible in B. Constructs that establish happens-before include: writing a `volatile` field (happens-before subsequent reads), releasing a `synchronized` lock (happens-before subsequent acquires), `Thread.start()` (happens-before the thread's first action), and `Thread.join()` (the thread's last action happens-before join returns). Without establishing happens-before, you have no guarantees — and that's how the trickiest concurrency bugs are born.

**Q3: What is a race condition, and how do you fix one?**

**A:** A race condition happens when two or more threads read-modify-write shared state without coordination, so the final result depends on unpredictable thread interleaving. Classic example: two threads both execute `counter++`. That's actually three operations — read counter, add one, write counter. If both threads read the old value at the same time, both add one to the *same* old value, both write back — one update is lost. **Fixes** (in order of preference): (1) **Eliminate the sharing** — use per-thread state or immutable data. (2) **Use atomic operations** — `AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()` does the whole read-add-write in one hardware step. (3) **Use synchronization** — wrap the compound operation in `synchronized (lock) { counter++; }`. The lesson: any read-modify-write on shared mutable state must be either atomic or locked.

**Q4: What is a deadlock, and how do you prevent it?**

**A:** A deadlock happens when two or more threads each hold a resource the other needs, and none can proceed. The four Coffman conditions: **mutual exclusion** (a resource is exclusively held), **hold-and-wait** (a thread holds one while requesting another), **no preemption** (resources can't be forcibly taken), and **circular wait** (a cycle of threads waiting on each other). Break any one to prevent deadlock. The most practical fix is **eliminating circular wait** by acquiring locks in a **globally consistent order** — if every thread grabs lock A before lock B, no cycle can form. Other techniques: use `tryLock` with a timeout, keep critical sections short, and prefer high-level primitives (executors, atomic operations, immutable data) over hand-rolled locking. And when a deadlock does happen in production, `jstack` shows you the exact locks each thread holds and waits for.

**Q5: Why use a thread pool instead of `new Thread()`?**

**A:** Creating an OS thread is expensive — a few milliseconds and ~1 MB of stack per thread. In a server handling 1000 requests/second, spawning one thread per request either exhausts memory or crashes with `OutOfMemoryError: unable to create native thread`. A **thread pool** reuses a small pool of threads for many tasks. `Executors.newFixedThreadPool(N)` maintains N threads; new tasks queue up when all are busy. This bounds resource usage and eliminates thread-creation cost. Modern Java 21 also offers **virtual threads** — millions of cheap threads managed by the JVM, so 'one thread per task' actually works for I/O work. But for CPU-bound work, a fixed pool sized to your core count remains the right choice.

**Q6: Runnable vs Callable vs Future vs CompletableFuture — what's each for?**

**A:** **`Runnable`** — a task that returns nothing (`void run()`). Can't throw checked exceptions. **`Callable<T>`** — a task that returns a value (`T call()`) and can throw. Submit both to an `ExecutorService`. **`Future<T>`** — a handle to a pending Callable result. `future.get()` blocks until it's done. Old and limited — no chaining. **`CompletableFuture<T>`** (Java 8+) — a Future you can chain: `.thenApply`, `.thenCompose`, `.exceptionally`, `.allOf`. Composable, non-blocking, and the modern way to write async code. Rule: `Runnable`/`Callable` for tasks, `CompletableFuture` for anything you'd otherwise nest callbacks or block on.

**Q7: What is a `ReentrantLock` and when would you prefer it over `synchronized`?**

**A:** `ReentrantLock` is a lock class in `java.util.concurrent.locks` that offers everything `synchronized` does plus: **`tryLock()`** (attempt without blocking, with optional timeout), **fairness** (grant locks in FIFO order), **interruptible waits** (a blocked thread can be `interrupt()`ed out), and **explicit Condition objects** (finer-grained wait/notify). Reach for it when `synchronized` isn't flexible enough — for example, a lock that needs a timeout to avoid deadlock, or a producer/consumer with multiple wait conditions. It requires more care (must `unlock()` in a `finally` block) but is more powerful. For simple mutual exclusion, `synchronized` is still cleaner and often faster.

## Real-world examples

- **Every HTTP server** processes concurrent requests via a thread pool. Bad tuning here is the #1 cause of server slowness.
- **Spring's `@Async` annotation** submits methods to an `ExecutorService` — you get async behavior with one annotation.
- **Kafka consumers** run in worker pools; a race on offset commits can cause double-processing or lost messages.
- **Android's `Handler` and Flutter's isolates** are alternatives to shared-memory threading — message passing instead of locks.
- **When production hangs and `jstack` shows two threads waiting on each other's locks** — that's a real deadlock. You'll fix it by imposing a global lock order.

## Advanced notes

- **Lock-free data structures** using CAS (e.g., `ConcurrentLinkedQueue`) — fast under contention but tricky to reason about.
- **ABA problem** — CAS sees 'A' twice and assumes nothing changed, but it went A→B→A. Fix with versioned references (`AtomicStampedReference`).
- **`ForkJoinPool`** — work-stealing pool used by parallel streams. Excellent for CPU-bound divide-and-conquer.
- **False sharing** — two threads writing to different variables that share a cache line kill each other's performance. Pad or align.
- **`StampedLock`** — optimistic reads for read-heavy state. Very fast when contention is low.
- **Structured concurrency (Java 21+ preview)** — treats concurrent tasks like structured programming: a scope opens; tasks inside are guaranteed to finish before the scope closes.

flashcards.back: volatile: visibility only (no atomicity). synchronized: visibility + mutual exclusion. Atomic: lock-free atomic read-modify-write via CAS.
A JMM guarantee — writes before a happens-before edge are visible after it. Established by volatile, synchronized, Thread.start/join, etc.
Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Break any one to prevent deadlock.
Threads are expensive (~1 MB stack, ms startup). Pools reuse threads and bound resource usage.
Runnable returns nothing, can't throw checked. Callable returns a value and can throw. Both submitted to ExecutorService.

quiz.explain: AtomicInteger uses hardware CAS — lock-free, fast, atomic.

quiz.q: Preferred way to run many short tasks?
quiz.explain: Pool reuses threads and bounds resources.
Mutex + hold-and-wait + no preemption + circular wait.

quiz.q: Two threads deadlock most often when…
options: They acquire the same locks in different orders

challenges.text: Spawn 8 threads incrementing a shared int 100,000 times each. Show the racy total; fix with AtomicInteger.
Print `Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()`; spawn that many threads, each print thread name + a random number.
CompletableFuture pipeline: fetch a user, then their orders, then their most expensive order.
Producer/consumer with BlockingQueue — producer + 2 consumers; verify no items lost.
Deadlock two threads intentionally (two locks, opposite order). Fix with consistent lock ordering.
Reduce a hot-spot: replace a global `synchronized` counter with `AtomicLong` and benchmark the throughput improvement.
Build a mini thread pool with BlockingQueue + N workers matching ExecutorService.submit API.
